name: Cabezo de la Mata — circular by the S-2 Barranco del Apio trail
name_original: Cabezo de la Mata — circular por el sendero S-2 Barranco del Apio
region_intro: Jogging in Moncayo offers a diverse landscape for runners. The region is characterized by significant elevation changes, with many challenging jogging trails leading through mountainous terrain and deep ravines. Runners can expect routes that traverse peaks and hills, providing varied Moncayo running trail difficulty levels. These Moncayo rutas often feature steep ascents and descents, making them suitable for experienced trail runners. Best jogging routes in Moncayo The most popular jogging route is Moncayo Circular Trail, a 6.1 miles (9.9 km) trail that takes 2 hours 3 minutes to complete. This difficult route features substantial elevation gain through varied mountain landscapes. Another top favourite among local runners is Cabezo de la Mata — circular by the S-2 Barranco del Apio trail, a moderate 3.2 miles (5.1 km) path. This route offers a less strenuous option, winding through a ravine with moderate climbs. Local runners also love the Cerro de San Juan loop from Parking Peña Roya, an 8.7 miles (13.9 km) trail leading through mountainous terrain, often completed in about 2 hours 33 minutes. Jogging in Moncayo is defined by significant elevation changes, mountain peaks, and deep ravines. The network offers options for different ability levels, though many routes present a considerable challenge. The guide includes highly-rated routes that showcase the best of the region's landscape. More than 650 runners have used komoot to explore Moncayo's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes are available in Moncayo?
A: There are over 10 dedicated running routes in Moncayo, offering a variety of experiences for trail runners. The region is known for its challenging terrain, with most routes featuring significant elevation changes.

Q: Are there any easy or beginner-friendly jogging routes in Moncayo?
A: Moncayo is primarily known for its challenging mountainous terrain. While most routes are difficult, there is one moderate option: the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e983199672/cabezo-de-la-mata-circular-by-the-s-2-barranco-del-apio-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabezo de la Mata — circular by the S-2 Barranco del Apio trail&lt;/a&gt;. This 3.2-mile (5.1 km) path offers a less strenuous experience through a ravine with moderate climbs, making it suitable for those looking for a slightly easier run.

Q: What is the typical distance for running trails in Moncayo?
A: Running routes in Moncayo vary significantly in distance. You can find shorter paths like the 3.2-mile (5.1 km) &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e983199672/cabezo-de-la-mata-circular-by-the-s-2-barranco-del-apio-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabezo de la Mata — circular&lt;/a&gt;, up to longer, more demanding trails such as the 15.6-mile (25.1 km) &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10464011" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Lobera loop from Parking Peña Roya&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Are there circular running routes in Moncayo?
A: Yes, Moncayo offers several excellent circular running routes. Popular options include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e983203586/moncayo-circular-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Moncayo Circular Trail&lt;/a&gt;, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e983199672/cabezo-de-la-mata-circular-by-the-s-2-barranco-del-apio-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabezo de la Mata — circular&lt;/a&gt;, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/19882936" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cerro de San Juan loop from Parking Peña Roya&lt;/a&gt;. These loops allow you to start and finish at the same point, often providing diverse scenery along the way.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on Moncayo's running trails?
A: Moncayo's running trails are characterized by significant elevation changes, leading through mountainous terrain, peaks, and deep ravines. Runners should expect varied surfaces, including rocky paths and forest trails, often with steep ascents and descents. The region is ideal for experienced trail runners seeking a challenge.

Q: What are some notable landmarks or natural attractions to see while running in Moncayo?
A: While running in Moncayo, you can encounter several impressive natural attractions. Highlights include the majestic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3924334" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Moncayo Summit&lt;/a&gt;, offering panoramic views, the intriguing &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5288045" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cueva de los Pilares&lt;/a&gt;, and the scenic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5271458" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Valcongosto Gorge&lt;/a&gt;. Many routes also pass by viewpoints like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5291398" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabezo de la Mata&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Is there parking available near the running trails in Moncayo?
A: Yes, some popular trailheads offer parking. For instance, routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/19882936" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cerro de San Juan loop&lt;/a&gt; and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/10464011" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Pico Lobera loop&lt;/a&gt; start from Parking Peña Roya, indicating designated parking facilities in that area. It's advisable to check specific route details for parking information.

Q: What is the best time of year to go jogging in Moncayo?
A: The best time for jogging in Moncayo is typically during spring (April-June) and autumn (September-October). During these seasons, temperatures are milder and the natural scenery is vibrant. Summer can be very hot, especially at lower elevations, while winter may bring snow and ice, making some higher trails inaccessible or more challenging.

Q: Are Moncayo's running trails suitable for families with children?
A: Given the significant elevation changes and the prevalence of difficult routes, most running trails in Moncayo are not ideal for young children or families seeking an easy stroll. The moderate &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e983199672/cabezo-de-la-mata-circular-by-the-s-2-barranco-del-apio-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cabezo de la Mata — circular&lt;/a&gt; might be an option for active families with older children accustomed to hiking, but caution is advised due to the terrain.

Q: What should I wear for running in Moncayo's mountainous environment?
A: For running in Moncayo's mountainous terrain, it's crucial to wear appropriate gear. This includes sturdy trail running shoes with good grip, moisture-wicking layers, and a windproof/waterproof jacket, as weather conditions can change rapidly. Consider carrying a hydration pack, snacks, and sun protection, especially for longer routes with significant exposure.

Q: What do other runners enjoy most about jogging in Moncayo?
A: Runners frequently praise Moncayo for its challenging and rewarding mountain landscapes. The diverse terrain, significant elevation gains, and stunning views from peaks like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3924334" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Moncayo Summit&lt;/a&gt; are often highlighted. The sense of accomplishment after tackling difficult routes through deep ravines is a common theme among those who explore the region.

Q: Are dogs allowed on the running trails in Moncayo?
A: Regulations regarding dogs on trails can vary within natural parks and protected areas like Moncayo. While many trails may permit dogs, it's always best to check local signage or the specific rules of the Moncayo Natural Park before heading out. Keep dogs on a leash, especially in areas with wildlife or livestock, and always clean up after your pet.
tips:
Cabezo de la Mata is a peak located in the Sierra del Moncayo, in Aragon, Spain. With an altitude of 1,437 metres, it is one of the most accessible peaks in the area.

One of the most popular routes to ascend Cabezo de la Mata begins at Fuente de los Frailes. This circular route offers spectacular views of the northeastern slope of the Sierra del Moncayo and the plain of the Ebro Valley. The route includes sections through beech and pine forests, and is suitable for all year round.
